coredata.dc:title: Luminescent mesoporous silica nanoparticles for biomedical applications: Synthesis and characterization
coredata.dc:creator: Jain, Beena
K.V, Reeja
Mondal, Puspen
Sinha, Anil Kumar
coredata.dc:description: We report the synthesis and detailed characterization of intrinsically/lable-free luminescent mesoporous silica nanoparticles (L-MSN), which may have useful biomedical applications. These particles were prepared by modified Stober's method followed by calcination. By optimizing the ratio of (3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane) APTS to tetraetthoxysaline (TEOS), particles with size ~ 43 nm and size dispersion ~ 12% were obtained. The luminescence of L-MSN is suggested to be due to the formation of carbonaceous compounds, giving particles yellowish brown color, during the process of calcination at optimum temperatures ~ 400 °C. Interestingly, our studies suggest that these carbonaceous impurities are carbon dots (C-Dots) and fluorescence anisotropy decay measurements indicate C-Dots as integral part of L-MSN. Luminescent properties of L-MSN were found to be stable as a function of pH as well as long time light exposure. Our results suggest that L-MSN may be explored for biomedical imaging as well as drug delivery applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescent mesoporous silica nanoparticles
C-Dots
Fluorescence anisotropy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Luminescent mesoporous silica nanoparticles (L-MSN)
Size, functional groups and structural characterization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Size of the L-MSN (i) measured using DLS as ~ 43 nm, with polydispersity ~ 12%, (ii) TEM image showing size and morphology (in the inset) of MSN, (iii) Color of MSN (a) yellowish brown of L-MSN (b) white of B-MSN.

(a) FTIR spectra of MSN. Inset shows additional C-H symmetric and asymmetric stretch at 2880 cm−1 and 2930 cm−1 in L-MSN; (b) SAD and (c) SXRD of the L-MSN. Diffused ring in Fig. 2b and broad peaks in Fig. 2c show amorphous nature of the particles.

(a) UV–Visible absorption spectra of B-MSN and L-MSN. No clear absorption features in spectra of B-MSN and L-MSN are seen due to scattering; (b) Excitation spectra of B-MSN and L-MSN at emission wavelength 520 nm.
(a) Absorbance of L-MSN and HF etched L-MSN suspended in buffer. Hump and shoulder at 280 nm and 350 nm is shown by arrow; (b) Emission spectra of L-MSN and HF treated L-MSN.
(a) Excitation wavelength dependent emission spectra of L-MSN; (b) Fluorescence lifetime decay of L-MSN. Fitted to multiexponential decay with average lifetime ~ 3.9 ns.
Fluorescence anisotropy decay of L-MSN and HF etched L-MSN (C-Dots) suspended in pH 7 buffer excited at 397 nm and monitored at 500 nm. Details are given in Table 1.
Stern-Volmer plot. The y-axis is the ratio of the unquenched fluorescence intensity F0 divided by the quenched fluorescence intensity F. The x-axis is the quencher (KI) concentration.

pH dependent emission of L-MSN with pH varying from 5.5 to 8.5 (a) intensity; (b) average fluorescence lifetime excited at 380 nm; (c) Kinetic scans of C-Dots and L-MSN irradiated at 9 mW/cm2 at 380 ± 20 nm.
Fluorescence anisotropy decay parameters of C-Dots and L-MSN suspended in buffer at pH 7.

head.ce:title: Luminescent mesoporous silica nanoparticles for biomedical applications: Synthesis and characterization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Raja Ramanna Center for Advanced Technology, Indore, M.P. 452013, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Raja Ramanna Center for Advanced Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the synthesis and detailed characterization of intrinsically/lable-free luminescent mesoporous silica nanoparticles (L-MSN), which may have useful biomedical applications. These particles were prepared by modified Stober's method followed by calcination. By optimizing the ratio of (3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane) APTS to tetraetthoxysaline (TEOS), particles with size ~ 43 nm and size dispersion ~ 12% were obtained. The luminescence of L-MSN is suggested to be due to the formation of carbonaceous compounds, giving particles yellowish brown color, during the process of calcination at optimum temperatures ~ 400 °C. Interestingly, our studies suggest that these carbonaceous impurities are carbon dots (C-Dots) and fluorescence anisotropy decay measurements indicate C-Dots as integral part of L-MSN. Luminescent properties of L-MSN were found to be stable as a function of pH as well as long time light exposure. Our results suggest that L-MSN may be explored for biomedical imaging as well as drug delivery applications.

We report the synthesis and detailed spectroscopic characterization of intrinsically/lable-free luminescent mesoporous silica nanoparticles (L-MSN) with low size dispersion which may have useful biomedical applications. Our studies suggest that the luminescence of L-MSN is due to the formation of carbon dots (C-Dots) which are the integral part of L-MSN as shown by fluorescence anisotropy measurements. Luminescence is found to be stable as a function of pH as well as long time light exposure. L-MSN may be explored for biomedical imaging as well as drug delivery applications. Fluorescence anisotropy decay of C-Dots and L-MSN suspended in buffer at pH 7
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: There has been a considerable interest to use mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN) for biomedical applications such as drug delivery and imaging [1]. This is due to their versatile properties. MSN have nanosized pores, which help them to be loaded with desired molecules such as drugs, at the same time, these are reported to be biocompatible and have rich surface chemistry which allows easy conjugation with different ligands for targeting [2]. In addition, optical transparency makes them useful for optical imaging using appropriate external probes [3]. However, these external probes/fluorophores are generally expensive and there are concerns about high toxicity, leaking and/or photo bleaching of these probes, which hampers long time monitoring [2,4,5]. Interestingly, label free mesoporous luminescent silica nanoparticles have been reported to be synthesized recently, where the luminescence is associated to the generation of carbonaceous impurities during the calcination process when in addition to the normally used tetraetthoxysaline (TEOS), silica precursors such as 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTS) is also used [6–8]. By modifying the experimental procedures and optimizing the ratio of APTS to TEOS, we obtained particles with smaller size and lower size dispersion than the reported ones, which is desired for biomedical applications [9,10]. In this paper we report the synthesis and detailed characterization of these intrinsically/label-free luminescent mesoporous silica nanoparticles (L-MSN), which may be useful for biomedical applications. Size and morphology of the NPs were studied by TEM and DLS technique. Optical properties of L-MSN such as absorption, and emission as a function of pH and fluorescence life time and anisotropy were studied. Interestingly, our studies suggest that these carbonaceous impurities are carbon dots (C-Dots) and fluorescence anisotropy decay measurements indicate C-Dots as integral part of L-MSN. This may ensure the co-localized probing of the delivered drug during the process of biomedical imaging.

Spectroscopic grade tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTS) were obtained from Acros chemicals and used without further purification. Surfactant cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), co-surfactant ethyl acetate, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), toluene and other solvents were purchased from Sigma and used as received. Water used was Millipore water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Luminescent mesoporous silica nanoparticles (L-MSN)
ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent mesoporous silica nanoparticles (L-MSN) were prepared by modified Stöber method following the paper by H.Chen et. al., with some modifications [8]. Briefly, 0.1 g CTAB was dissolved in 45 mL of water and stirred for 10 min at 1150 RPM. To this 0.6 mL of 2 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was added and heated up to 80 °C under continued stirring. Within 30 min 1.5 mL ethyl acetate was added. After 1 h, 1.4 mL tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) was added to the reaction mixture and subsequently, 200 µL of 3-aminoprpyl triethoxysilane (APTES) was added drop by drop. The stirring was continued at the same temperature for 2 h. After it cooled down, the suspension was kept for dialysis to remove surfactant and other non-reacted products. Dialyzed particles were centrifuged at 16,000 RPM for 5 min at 25 °C. Supernatant was removed and the sediment again was washed twice with ethanol and ethanol water mixture and centrifuged for 5 min. The raw product was re-dispersed in ethanol. Further, 5 µL of HCl was added to this solution and stirred for 3 h at 60 °C. Again particles were washed twice with ethanol and centrifuged for 5 min at 25 °C and subsequently dried at 60 °C over night. Powder was kept for calcination at 400 °C for 2 h. At the end L-MSN with yellowish brownish color were obtained.
Bare (non-fluorescent) mesoporous silica nanoparticles (B-MSN) were also prepared. The methodology was same as used to prepare L-MSN except the addition of APTES.

MSN in colloidal form were obtained after ultra sonicating the powder for ~ 15 min in 10 mM sodium phosphate buffer at pH 5.5, 7.4 and 9.5. The final concentration of 5 mg/mL was kept for experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size, functional groups and structural characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Size and morphology of the NPs were studied by TEM and DLS technique. TEM imaging of L-MSN was done using TEM model CM200 from Philips, operating at 200 kV. Samples for TEM were prepared by dipping the carbon coated copper grid in the nanoparticles solution and evaporating in the dark. This process was repeated a few times to get sufficient number of NPs in the field of view. Selective area diffraction (SAD) was done along with the TEM measurements. Hydrodynamic sizes and Zeta potential of the particles were obtained by dynamic light scattering method using 90 Plus, Brookhaven Instrument Corporation.

Functional groups of MSNs were characterized using FTIR spectromter FTLA 2000 MB 104 from ABB Bomem. The spectra of the powdered samples were obtained using a single reflection ATR (MIRacle using diamond coated ZnSe plate, from Pike Technologies) at 4 cm−1 resolution. For each sample, an average of at least 60 scans was done to obtain good S/N ratio. X-ray diffraction studies were done to find if carbon dots inside the L-MSN-could be of crystalline nature using selected area diffraction (SAD) and synchrotron X-ray diffraction (SXRD, wavelength ~ 0.8 Å). SXRD was done using ADXRD beamline (BL-12) at Indus-2 synchrotron source.

Absorption and emission spectra of colloidal L-MSN was recorded using Cintra 20 spectrophotometer (GBC, Australia) with 1 nm resolution and Fluorolog-2 spectrofluorometer (Spex, USA) with 4 nm resolution respectively.

Fluorescence lifetime of L-MSN was recorded using a time correlated single photon counting module (Lifespec-RED from Edinburgh Instruments) having MCP PMT (R3809U-50) as detector. The excitation source was the second harmonic of the femto second laser (Verdi-Mira combination) having wavelength~ 385 nm, with 200 fs pulse width. The instrument response function of the system was ~ 50 ps details are described in ref [11]. A 390 nm high-pass filter was placed at the collection side before the monochromator to minimize the excitation light signal. The lifetime values of the particles were calculated by using the software supplied by the manufacturer. The goodness of the fit was judged by the chi-square values and by visual inspection of the residuals. Time resolved fluorescence anisotropy was measured by rotating the emission polarizer between 0 and 90 degree with respect to the excitation pulse at least 20 times such that peak counts ~ 40,000 were obtained in the 0 degree polarization. The fitting was done using the software provided by the supplier in the tail fitting mode. The goodness of the fit was judged again by the chi-square values and by visual inspection of the residuals.

For photobleaching studies, 370–400 nm bandpass filter was used to select the wavelength of irradiation from the broadband output of a xenon lamp source. The samples were kept at a distance of 30 cm from the light source and the power incident at the sample was 9 mW/cm2 measured by OPHIR power meter.

MSN were synthesized following the procedure as described in Section 2.1. The size of L-MSN was optimized by varying the ratio of APTS to TEOS. At APTS/TEOS ratio ~ 14%, MSN with size and dispersion smaller as compared to the published reports were obtained [7,8]. Fig. 1 shows the size and morphology of the L-MSN as measured using DLS and TEM. Both the techniques show the size for MSN ~ 43 nm with the polydispersity ~ 0.12. Surface of the MSN was found to be porous as shown be high resolution TEM image (inset of Fig. 1(ii)). The zeta potential was obtained as ~ − 45 mV ± 5 mV at physiological pH. This shows that MSN form stable suspension with negatively charged surface, which may be due to the presence of silanol groups on the surface. This suspension was found to be stable for more than a year. Fig. 1(iii) shows the color of L-MSN as yellowish brown whereas that of B-MSN, where no APTS was used, as white. Similar colored L-MSN has been synthesized by other groups [6–8]. The color is suggested to be due to the presence carbonaceous impurities in the L-MSN generated after calcination [6,8]. This is supported by the FTIR absorbance spectra of the L-MSN and B-MSN as shown in Fig. 2a. In addition to Si-O vibration peaks at 1090 and 800 cm−1, the presence of C-H symmetry and asymmetry stretch peaks at 2885 and 2930 cm−1 (inset of Fig. 2a) in L-MSN indicate the presence of hydrocarbon compounds in the L-MSN whereas in the B-MSN these peaks are absent. In order to know whether these carbon compounds formed in the L-MSN are crystalline or amorphous, SAD and SXRD measurements were carried out [12]. SAD pattern of L-MSN is shown in Fig. 2b. Diffused circular rings indicate that the matrix of MSN is amorphous. Surface X-ray diffraction (SXRD) also shows broad peaks (Fig. 2c). Both SAD and SXRD are probably dominated by the amorphous nature of silica of L-MSN and hence do not reveal any extra information about the carbon compounds embedded in the silica matrix. However, Kwon et al. observed short range order in C-Dots as typical diffraction pattern of graphitic carbon [12].

UV–Visible absorbance spectra of colloidal MSN suspended in 10 mM buffer are shown in Fig. 3a. For both the MSN, the spectra are dominated by particle scattering hence, no absorption features are seen. Interestingly, the excitation spectrum, in Fig. 3b, shows an order of magnitude higher emission intensity of L-MSN as compared to B-MSN. This emission indicates that there are fluorescent compounds present in the L-MSN. Several explanations have been suggested for the cause of emission in L-MSN by different research groups [6,12,13], accordingly, emission in L-MSN could be due to several reasons. These include generation of carbon compounds during the process of calcinations or the defects generated in the silica matrix, etc. For example, Jacob et al. have suggested the cause of emission to be the introduction of carbon and oxygen defects in the silica network due to the calcination of aminopropyl groups [6]. Also, according to the earlier published reports, amorphous silica even in pure form, depending on the ambient conditions such as wetting or heating at high temperatures in oxygen/hydrogen atmosphere, gives luminescence when exposed to the ultraviolet light [13]. Porous silica matrix of nanoparticles, generated by reaction of a variety of organic acids with alkoxy silane, also shows a broad band luminescence [13–15].

In our case, however, no significant emission was observed from L-MSN before calcination. To find the role of amorphous silica matrix to give luminescence, L-MSN was dissolved in hydrogen fluoride (HF) and absorption and emission was studied. The absorbance of L-MSN etched with HF, in Fig. 4a, shows a hump at 280 nm and shoulder at 350 nm. Similar absorbance features are reported by other groups which have been associated to the presence of C-Dots [14,16]. The emission spectra are shown in Fig. 4b. While the emission of both, L-MSN and HF treated L-MSN, is comparable, it is more than 50 times higher than that of B-MSN. Thus, we find that, even after treatment with HF, where the silica network should no longer be retained, luminescence characteristics of the system are still retained with quantum yield ~ 2.6%.

Fig. 5a shows the excitation wavelength dependent emission spectra of L-MSN. Similar nature of excited wavelength dependent emission is reported by other groups for C-Dots [8,16]. Thus, these characteristic absorption and emission spectral features can be attributed to the presence of C-Dots in L-MSN, which are believed to be generated due to the process of calcination. The origin of C-Dot emission is not very clear and is still a matter of debate [17,18]. Depending on the type of C-Dots, it could be from the carbon-core and surface states. The emission due to carbon-core is mostly characterized by conjugated π-domains and gets effect of quantum confinement due to nano size while due to surface states are related to hybridization of the carbon backbone and connected functional groups or fluorescent molecules connected on the surface or inner of the C-Dots or surface defects. In our case the emission may be related either to carbon/oxygen or carbon/nitrogen related surface energy states resulting from calcinations of the APTES containing spheres or carbon core [6]. This is also consistent with the absorption features seen in Fig. 4a. The observed broad emission (in Fig. 4 and Fig. 5) may be attributed to the heterogeneity in C-Dots size and surface states.

The fluorescence decay of L-MSN is found to be multiexponential (shown in Fig. 5b), with average lifetimes, in buffer as 3.9 ± 0.1 ns. Similar fluorescence decay times have been reported for C-Dots fluorescence also [19]. For biomedical imaging it is important that the particle carrying the drug is easily located. This requires that the fluorophore/signalling probe is the integral part of the particle. For L-MSN, this was investigated by studying its fluorescence anisotropy decay. In Fig. 6 the fluorescence anisotropy decay of L-MSN suspended in buffer is shown. For comparison, anisotropy decay of L-MSN etched with HF is also shown. The data is fitted satisfactorily to an exponential decay model as:
r
( t )
=
r
0
[
a
1
e − t /
τ
1
+
a
2
e − t /
τ
2
]
,
where r
0 is the initial anisotropy and τ
1 and τ
2 are the time constants for the decay of r
0 having pre-exponential factors a1 and a2 respectively. While anisotropy decay for HF etched L-MSN i.e., C-Dots (suspended in buffer) is best fitted as single exponential, two exponential decay is obtained for L-MSN suspension in buffer. Fitting parameters are shown in Table 1. The component τ
1 represents the overall factors responsible for faster dephasing of anisotropy including local motion of the free C-Dots or bound C-Dots wobbling within the solvent shell around the nanoparticle whereas the slower component τ
2 represents the overall motion of the bound C-Dots-MSN complex. Single component decay time τ
1
~ 0.2 ns obtained in case of HF etched L-MSN i.e., C-Dots indicates that fluorescent particles are very small and independent and thus depolarization is very fast. The anisotropy decay of L-MSN is fitted to two components. Faster component has decay time ~ 0.3 ns i.e., of same order as of HF etched L-MSN C-Dots accounting for 26% of anisotropy decay. Little higher value of this fast decay component as compared to that of HF etched L-MSN may account for bound C-Dots wobbling within the solvent shell in pores or around the MSN. Longer decay component accounting for major anisotropy decay has decay time ~ 200 ns. We note that the experimental time window is not sufficient enough to give correct estimation of the overall motion of L-MSN. As C-Dots are the only fluorescent moieties, the long component of anisotropy decay in L-MSN clearly indicate that C-Dots are rigidly bound to/embedded in L-MSN and rotate along with MSN making the overall dephasing time longer.

The location of the fluorescent centres on the L-MSN was investigated by fluorescence quenching studies. Fig. 7 shows the Stern-Volmer plot, where the decrease of fluorescence is plotted against concentration of iodine. In the figure, the initial part is linear (shown in inset) which gradually saturates. The Stern-Volmer quenching constant, Kq, for L-MSN, resulting from the linear fitting of the curve, for low iodine concentrations, is obtained as 7.2 whereas for C-Dots after HF etching is obtained as 10.8. Thus, the value of Kq is larger for the exposed luminescent emitters than for the buried ones. While the linear part suggests the availability of fluorophores on the surface of the L-MSN, large luminescence even in the saturation region indicates that fluorescent centres are present inside the NPs also which is not affected by the environment.

Stability of the fluorescence against light irradiation as well as pH was also checked as it is essential for these MSN to be used for imaging studies. For this, L-MSN as well as C-dot suspension in buffer was irradiated at 380 ± 20 nm for more than 10 h with intensity ranging from 5 to 30 mW/cm2. No degradation of emission intensity of particles with time was observed as shown in Fig. 8a indicating no photobleaching. The effect of pH, varying from 5.5 to 8.5, on the emission characteristics was studied in both steady state and time resolved regime, as shown in Figs. 8b and 8c. The emission spectra as well as fluorescence decay time of L-MSN show no significant change in the spectral characteristics as well as lifetime.

Although, excitation and emission of L-MSN is out of biological window (700–1400 nm), these nanoparticles may be used for histology settings such as immunofluorescence investigations. It is possible to use their fluorescence for bio distribution analysis as the fluorescence is stable against long time exposure and environmental pH. Thus, our study suggests that L-MSN may be explored for biomedical imaging in an environment even with variable pH as well as drug delivery applications.

We prepared luminescent mesoporous silica nanoparticles with modified Stober's method. Luminescence is suggested to be due to the formation of C-Dots generated during the process of calcination at an optimum temperature. These C-dots are found to be the integral part of the L-MSN and their emission is independent of the light exposure and pH. Thus L-MSN can be explored for imaging as well as drug delivery applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Dr. P.K.Gupta and Dr. S.K. Majumder for their support to carry out this work.
